refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, protocol choice, or network architecture. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days of operational use). Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Pass if measured availability ≥ target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative communication channels before loss of availability occurs.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth management and protocol selection that dynamically adjusts communication parameters to maintain minimum viable link availability under varying network conditions.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) with quality monitoring (DP2) and local fallback capability (DP3) in an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated switching logic.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (a) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (b) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (c) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback mode, coordinated by a unified switching and state management logic.

potential_sources: DP5 couples three sub-solutions (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) but maintains logical independence through clear switching boundaries: monitoring informs failover decisions without prescribing pathway technology; fallback mode operates independently of pathway selection; pathway redundancy is agnostic to monitoring implementation.
Risk of tight coupling between failover logic and local fallback mode if state synchronization is not cleanly abstracted—mitigated by explicit state machine definition at DP level.

Monitoring subsystem must not create circular dependencies with failover (e.g., monitoring system itself becoming a single point of failure)—addressed by requiring monitoring to be distributed and independent of primary/backup pathways.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is preferred over single-solution candidates (DP1–DP4) because 0.999 availability (≤8.76 hours downtime/year) is difficult to achieve with any single mechanism alone. Redundancy alone (DP1) does not guarantee detection speed; monitoring alone (DP2) cannot prevent all failures; fallback alone (DP3) limits operational capability. Integration is justified by the stringent availability target and the need for defense-in-depth. Coupling is acceptable because the three mechanisms operate at different layers (transport, detection, operational mode) with well-defined handoff points.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold is ≥0.999 and is technically achievable given worksite network infrastructure and operational constraints. RISK: If actual infrastructure cannot support this (e.g., single ISP, no local fallback capability), DP5 may be infeasible. MITIGATION: Conduct infrastructure audit and baseline availability measurement before design commitment.

ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous fallback mode is operationally acceptable (i.e., reduced functionality is safer than no control). RISK: If worksite safety plan prohibits any autonomous operation, fallback mode is invalid. MITIGATION: Align DP5 with safety plan early; if fallback is prohibited, revert to DP1+DP2 (redundancy + monitoring only).

ASSUMPTION: Redundant pathways can be provisioned independently (e.g., different carriers, technologies, or network segments). RISK: If worksite has only one physical network infrastructure, true redundancy is impossible. MITIGATION: Define 'redundancy' broadly (e.g., temporal diversity, protocol diversity, or logical separation) if physical diversity is unavailable.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive degradation detection is feasible with available telemetry (latency, packet loss, jitter). RISK: If link degradation is sudden and unpredictable (e.g., RF interference, hardware failure), prediction may fail. MITIGATION: Combine prediction with reactive failover; set aggressive thresholds for reactive triggers.

ASSUMPTION: Switching logic and state management can be implemented with acceptable latency (<<1 second) to meet operational safety requirements. RISK: If switching introduces unacceptable control lag, operator safety is compromised. MITIGATION: Define maximum acceptable switching latency in FR refinement; validate in prototype testing.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is representative of normal operational conditions. RISK: If evaluation period excludes high-risk scenarios (e.g., peak load, adverse weather, maintenance windows), measured availability may not reflect real-world performance. MITIGATION: Define evaluation period to include representative operational scenarios; document exclusions.

ASSUMPTION: No single point of failure exists in the monitoring or switching subsystem itself. RISK: If monitoring system or failover controller fails, availability target is unmet. MITIGATION: Require monitoring and switching logic to be distributed, redundant, or embedded in independent modules.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'link availability' (e.g., round-trip latency <X ms, packet loss <Y%, or binary up/down); (2) Acceptable operational modes during link unavailability (e.g., local autonomous control, manual override, safe shutdown); (3) Maximum acceptable switching latency between primary and backup pathways; (4) Evaluation period scope and representative scenarios; (5) Baseline infrastructure capabilities (e.g., available network pathways, local processing capability, safety constraints). These refinements will constrain DP5 and enable rigorous IM (implementation module) definition and testing strategy.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Unified availability assurance system managing redundant communication pathways, link quality monitoring, failover coordination, and autonomous fallback modes for teleoperated vehicle control

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to physical/logical communication pathways; Teleoperation module monitors link quality, triggers failover, and switches between redundant pathways

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation ensures delivery via available pathways or queues during degradation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state and link quality metrics; supports predictive degradation detection and operator awareness

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical events when communication availability falls below thresholds or autonomous fallback is activated

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of communication availability, failover logic, and link quality monitoring. The unified switching and state management logic is core to teleoperation architecture. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces cross boundary; Network Link is a direct dependency.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could own failover and fallback logic from a safety perspective, but would not naturally own link quality monitoring or communication pathway management. High diffusion: redundant pathway switching and predictive degradation detection would leak into Teleoperation and Vehicle modules.

Poor fit: Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle dynamics and control, not communication infrastructure. Placing availability assurance here creates tight coupling between vehicle control and network management. High diffusion: switching logic, link monitoring, and failover coordination would diffuse across Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing DP5 here avoids specificity and creates ambiguity about ownership. Diffusion would be high across all child modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of availability assurance because: (1) it is responsible for maintaining operator-to-vehicle command and feedback loops; (2) redundant pathways, failover, and link quality monitoring are intrinsic to reliable teleoperation; (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback is a teleoperation-specific pattern; (4) unified switching and state management directly supports teleoperation state coherence; (5) all external interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are already teleoperation dependencies.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module has independent communication requirements (e.g., vehicle-to-infrastructure direct links), availability assurance logic may need to be partially replicated or shared, increasing coupling.
Risk: Safety Services may need to override or veto failover decisions in safety-critical scenarios; this requires a well-defined SafetyAlert interface and clear decision authority.

Risk: Predictive degradation detection may require machine learning or statistical models; if these are centralized elsewhere in NGHE Solution, Teleoperation becomes dependent on external prediction services, increasing latency.

Risk: Local autonomous fallback mode implies Vehicle module has some independent decision-making capability; coordination between Teleoperation and Vehicle fallback logic must be explicit to avoid conflicts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criterion is quantitative and measurable: link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period. Teleoperation module can instrument Network Link interface to collect availability metrics, log failover events, and report aggregated availability to external monitoring/compliance systems. Predictive degradation detection outputs can be logged and correlated with actual link failures to validate detection accuracy. SafetyAlert interface provides traceability of threshold violations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. Teleoperation module is unambiguously assigned ownership of availability assurance (containment_score 0.92). All four external interfaces are well-defined: Network Link (direct dependency for pathway switching), Tasking (input dependency for command delivery), Telemetry (output dependency for state/metrics), SafetyAlert (output dependency for threshold violations). Ownership boundaries are explicit and do not overlap with Vehicle or Safety Services primary responsibilities.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly and completely addresses FR requirement. The three pillars of DP5 (redundant pathways + failover, real-time monitoring + predictive detection, hierarchical architecture + autonomous fallback) are necessary and sufficient to maintain availability at or above target threshold. Unified switching and state management logic ensures coherent failover behavior. DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific technologies) and is decomposable into sub-DPs at next level (e.g., failover algorithm, link quality metrics, fallback decision logic).

diffusion_risks: MEDIUM: Vehicle module autonomous fallback mode creates a coordination boundary. If Vehicle has independent decision-making for fallback (e.g., safe-stop logic), Teleoperation and Vehicle fallback logic must be explicitly synchronized to avoid conflicting commands. Recommend explicit handshake protocol in Teleoperation<->Vehicle interface specification.

MEDIUM: Predictive degradation detection may require external ML/statistical services. If prediction models are centralized in a separate NGHE Solution analytics module, Teleoperation becomes dependent on external service latency. Recommend defining SLA for prediction service response time and fallback behavior if service is unavailable.

LOW: Safety Services may need to veto or override failover decisions in safety-critical scenarios (e.g., operator commands vehicle into hazardous state during link degradation). SafetyAlert interface is defined, but decision authority (who decides: Teleoperation or Safety Services?) must be explicit. Recommend adding SafetyAlert input interface to Teleoperation for safety veto signals.

LOW: If Vehicle module has independent communication requirements (e.g., vehicle-to-infrastructure direct links for sensor data), availability assurance logic may need partial replication. Recommend clarifying Vehicle module communication architecture in next-level decomposition to avoid hidden coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT containment fit (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural owner of communication availability because: (1) operator-to-vehicle command/feedback loops are core teleoperation responsibility; (2) redundant pathways, failover, and link quality monitoring are intrinsic to reliable teleoperation; (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback is a teleoperation-specific pattern; (4) unified switching and state management directly supports teleoperation state coherence; (5) all external interfaces are already teleoperation dependencies. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert and Tasking cross boundary; Network Link is direct dependency. Alternative allocations (Safety Services, Vehicle, NGHE Solution) show significantly lower containment scores (0.65, 0.45, 0.5 respectively) and higher diffusion risks.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Add explicit SafetyAlert input interface to Teleoperation module. Current SafetyAlert is output-only; recommend bidirectional interface to allow Safety Services to veto or override failover decisions in safety-critical scenarios. Define decision authority and conflict resolution logic.

REVISION 2: Clarify Vehicle module autonomous fallback mode coordination. Specify handshake protocol between Teleoperation and Vehicle for fallback activation/deactivation to prevent conflicting commands during link degradation. Document fallback decision hierarchy (e.g., Teleoperation initiates, Vehicle executes, Safety Services monitors).

REVISION 3: Define SLA for predictive degradation detection service. If prediction models are external to Teleoperation module, specify maximum latency, availability, and fallback behavior if service is unavailable. Recommend co-locating prediction logic in Teleoperation module if latency is critical (<100ms).

REVISION 4: Decompose DP5 into sub-DPs at next level: (a) DP5.1 Redundant pathway switching logic, (b) DP5.2 Real-time link quality monitoring and metrics aggregation, (c) DP5.3 Predictive degradation detection algorithm, (d) DP5.4 Hierarchical fallback decision logic, (e) DP5.5 Unified state management and coordination. Map each sub-DP to specific implementation modules (e.g., DP5.1 -> LinkSwitcher class, DP5.2 -> LinkMonitor class) to reduce diffusion risk.

REVISION 5: Add acceptance criterion for failover latency. Current criterion specifies availability threshold (≥0.999) but not failover response time. Recommend adding: 'Failover latency ≤ [X]ms for [Y]% of failover events' to ensure operator experience is acceptable during link degradation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. FR5 is well-allocated to Teleoperation module (containment 0.92), DP5 is solution-neutral and decomposable, and diffusion risks are identified but manageable with explicit interface protocols. However, the gate results surface five critical revisions that must be addressed BEFORE full next-level decomposition proceeds. The recommended revisions are architectural (not functional defects), but given HIGH safety criticality, they must be resolved at current level to prevent propagation of ambiguity into sub-FRs. Recommend ZIGZAG_DOWN with mandatory revision loop: (1) apply REVISION 1-5 to FR5/DP5/IM specifications, (2) re-validate FR-IM and DP-IM gates with revised interfaces, (3) then decompose into sub-FRs with clear ownership and coordination protocols.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Maintain redundant communication pathways (primary + fallback) with automatic switching capability to ensure operator commands reach vehicle within acceptable latency during primary link degradation.

FR5.2: Monitor real-time link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength) and aggregate availability data to enable quantitative reporting of link availability ≥ 0.999 over evaluation period.
FR5.3: Detect predictive degradation patterns in link quality trends to enable proactive failover before link failure occurs, with fallback to reactive failover if prediction service is unavailable.

FR5.4: Coordinate hierarchical fallback decision-making between Teleoperation (initiator), Vehicle (executor), and Safety Services (veto authority) to ensure safe autonomous operation during extended link loss without conflicting commands.

FR5.5: Maintain unified state management and synchronization across redundant pathways, failover logic, and Vehicle autonomous fallback mode to ensure operator awareness and command coherence during link transitions.

revise.why: Five mandatory revisions identified by gate analysis must be applied before decomposition proceeds: (1) FR5 and DP5 must explicitly include SafetyAlert input interface (bidirectional) to enable Safety Services veto authority in safety-critical scenarios—current specification is output-only and creates decision authority ambiguity. (2) DP5 must specify explicit handshake protocol between Teleoperation and Vehicle for fallback coordination to prevent conflicting autonomous decisions during link degradation. (3) DP5 must define SLA for predictive degradation detection service (latency, availability, fallback behavior) if external to Teleoperation module—critical for safety-critical failover timing. (4) IM specification must clarify Vehicle module communication architecture and independent fallback mode to avoid hidden coupling and replication of availability logic. (5) FR5 acceptance criterion must add failover latency requirement (e.g., ≤100ms for 95% of events) to ensure operator experience is acceptable during link transitions. These revisions are architectural (interface protocols, ownership boundaries, SLAs) not functional defects, but must be resolved at current level given HIGH safety criticality to prevent propagation into sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Teleoperation module has exclusive ownership of operator-to-vehicle communication availability assurance (FR5 allocation is stable and non-negotiable).
Vehicle module has independent autonomous fallback mode (safe-stop logic) that must be explicitly coordinated with Teleoperation failover logic via handshake protocol.
Safety Services module has veto authority over failover decisions in safety-critical scenarios (e.g., operator commands vehicle into hazardous state during link degradation).
Predictive degradation detection can be either co-located in Teleoperation module or external service; if external, SLA must be defined and fallback behavior specified.
Link availability ≥ 0.999 is measurable over agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days) via instrumentation of Network Link interface and failover event logging.
Failover latency is acceptable to operator experience (assumption: ≤100ms for 95% of events, but must be validated with stakeholders).
Redundant pathways (primary + fallback) are available in deployment environment (e.g., cellular + satellite, WiFi + LTE); if not, FR5 may be infeasible.
Real-time link quality monitoring does not introduce unacceptable overhead on Vehicle or Network Link modules (latency, bandwidth, power consumption).
open_risks: SAFETY-CRITICAL: Decision authority ambiguity between Teleoperation and Safety Services during failover. If Teleoperation initiates failover but Safety Services detects hazardous state, who decides final action? Recommend explicit conflict resolution logic (e.g., Safety Services veto overrides Teleoperation failover) before sub-FR decomposition.

COORDINATION: Vehicle autonomous fallback mode may conflict with Teleoperation failover logic if not explicitly synchronized. Example: Teleoperation initiates failover to fallback pathway while Vehicle simultaneously initiates safe-stop. Recommend explicit handshake protocol (e.g., Teleoperation sends 'FALLBACK_ACTIVE' signal, Vehicle acknowledges and suspends independent fallback logic) before DP5.4 decomposition.

LATENCY: Predictive degradation detection service latency may exceed failover response time requirement if external to Teleoperation module. If prediction latency >100ms, reactive failover may be only viable option. Recommend co-locating prediction logic in Teleoperation module or defining aggressive SLA (<50ms) for external service.

COUPLING: Vehicle module communication architecture (e.g., vehicle-to-infrastructure direct links for sensor data) may require partial replication of availability logic outside Teleoperation module. If Vehicle has independent communication requirements, availability assurance may diffuse across modules. Recommend clarifying Vehicle communication architecture in next-level decomposition.

VERIFICATION: Failover latency acceptance criterion is missing from FR5. Current criterion specifies availability threshold (≥0.999) but not response time. Operator experience during link degradation depends on failover latency; recommend adding quantitative criterion (e.g., ≤100ms for 95% of events) before verification planning.

EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY: If predictive degradation detection relies on external ML/statistical services (e.g., NGHE Solution analytics module), Teleoperation becomes dependent on external service availability and latency. If service is unavailable, fallback to reactive failover must be automatic and transparent. Recommend defining fallback behavior and testing service failure scenarios.
evidence_needed: Explicit interface specification for SafetyAlert (bidirectional): define signal types, timing, and decision authority (who decides: Teleoperation or Safety Services?) for safety veto scenarios.

Handshake protocol specification between Teleoperation and Vehicle for fallback coordination: define state machine, signal timing, and conflict resolution logic (e.g., Teleoperation initiates, Vehicle executes, Safety Services monitors).

SLA specification for predictive degradation detection service (if external): define maximum latency (<50ms recommended), availability (≥0.999), and fallback behavior if service is unavailable (e.g., switch to reactive failover).

Vehicle module communication architecture specification: clarify independent communication requirements (e.g., vehicle-to-infrastructure links), autonomous fallback mode logic, and coordination with Teleoperation failover.

Failover latency acceptance criterion: define quantitative requirement (e.g., ≤100ms for 95% of failover events) and measurement methodology (e.g., timestamp failover initiation and completion, aggregate over evaluation period).

Redundant pathway availability in deployment environment: confirm that primary + fallback pathways are available (e.g., cellular + satellite, WiFi + LTE) and that switching logic is feasible within latency budget.
Real-time link quality monitoring overhead analysis: quantify impact on Vehicle module (latency, bandwidth, power consumption) and confirm acceptable to Vehicle design constraints.
Operator experience validation: conduct user studies or simulation to confirm that failover latency ≤100ms is acceptable to operator and does not degrade teleoperation performance.
